title: Battenberg cake
subtitle: The pink and yellow chequered marzipan-wrapped sponge
excerpt: Four alternating squares of pink and yellow almond sponge, bound with apricot jam and wrapped in a thin sheet of marzipan. Made from a single batter divided and coloured, baked side by side in a divided tin.
sourceNotes: Battenberg cake is said to have been created for the 1884 marriage of Prince Louis of Battenberg. The chequerboard construction and marzipan wrap are documented in Mrs Beeton's later editions (revised 1890s onwards, successor editions). Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records it as an established British tea cake with the four-square pattern as the defining characteristic.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge can be baked a day ahead and assembled the following day. Refrigerate the assembled cake for up to two days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pliable paste made from ground almonds and sugar, used in baking for modelling, filling, and covering cakes.

content.text: Battenberg is a construction project as much as a baking exercise. The chequered interior comes from baking a single almond sponge batter in two halves side by side in a divided tin, one half tinted pink, then cutting each half into two strips and assembling them in opposite-colour pairs with apricot jam before wrapping the whole thing in
. The join is invisible once rolled and the cross-section only reveals itself when sliced.

A proper divided Battenberg tin makes the baking stage easier, but a standard square tin with a folded strip of foil or card down the centre works well. The critical part is cutting the cooled sponge strips to an exactly equal size so the checkerboard is neat when assembled.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper and divide the tin in half with a double-folded strip of foil. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in {{eggs}} gradually. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} and {{almond-extract}}. Divide the batter evenly between two bowls. Stir a drop of {{food-colouring}} into one half until evenly pink. Spoon the plain batter into one side of the divided tin and the pink into the other. Level both surfaces. Bake for 30–35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the tops spring back when pressed. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then on a rack.

Trim the top of each sponge level if domed. Cut each sponge lengthways into two equal strips, as long and as wide and as tall as possible. You now have two pink strips and two yellow strips. Brush one yellow strip with warm {{apricot-jam}} and set a pink strip alongside it; press them together. Brush the top surfaces of both strips with jam and place the remaining two strips on top in opposite colours to complete the checkerboard. Press firmly together. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm.

Dust the work surface with {{icing-sugar}}. Roll {{marzipan}} to a rectangle about 3 mm thick and large enough to wrap around all four sides and the two ends of the sponge block, leaving the cut ends exposed. Brush the outside of the assembled sponge with the remaining jam. Lay the sponge at one edge of the marzipan, roll it up, and press the join underneath. Trim the ends neatly and press them flat.
The sponge strips must be equal in height or the checkerboard will be uneven. A ruler and a sharp long knife give cleaner cuts than guessing.
Gel food colouring gives a more vivid pink than liquid colouring without thinning the batter.
The assembled cake keeps for three days in the fridge, wrapped. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before serving; cold marzipan is firm and waxy.
